summary: Global markets are displaying a cautiously optimistic mood, with Wall Street posting gains led by a recovery in chip stocks even as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and broader conflict zones keep investors on edge. The key macro drivers include escalating Houthi aggression targeting Saudi crude tankers in the Red Sea, reopening fears over oil supply disruptions, while Treasury Secretary Bessent's comments on reduced Chinese purchases of Iranian oil and gold's gains on de-escalation hopes suggest mixed signals on the US-Iran conflict trajectory. At the sector level, semiconductor stocks are rebounding, Halliburton beat earnings estimates on strong European and Latin American oilfield services demand, GM is pivoting back toward gas-powered vehicles signaling a broader EV strategy retreat, and European banks are positioned for a profit windfall from lending and trading activity. The primary risk factors investors are monitoring include Red Sea shipping disruptions threatening energy supply chains, the widening US-Iran conflict and its ripple effects on oil prices and regional stability, SpaceX's upcoming lock-up expiration introducing potential share dilution pressure, and Wall Street's retreat from rental housing ahead of new regulatory restrictions. In the short term, markets will remain volatile and headline-sensitive, with earnings results, oil price movements, and any further Houthi escalation against Saudi infrastructure likely to serve as the dominant catalysts for directional moves.
